class Solution {
public:
    int magicalString(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;
        if (n <= 3) {
            // For n=1, 2, 3, the first n elements are 1, 12, 122. All have 1 one.
            return 1;
        }
        vector<int> s{1,2,2};
        int current = 1; // next character to append (1 or 2)
        int i = 2; // index in s to read the count
        while (s.size() < n) {
            int count = s[i];
            for (int j = 0; j < count; ++j) {
                s.push_back(current);
            }
            current = 3 - current; // flip between 1 and 2
            ++i;
        }
        // count 1s in first n elements
        int ones = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (s[i] == 1) ++ones;
        }
        return ones;
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int magicalString(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;
        if (n <= 3) return 1;
        vector<int> s{1,2,2};
        int current = 1;
        int i = 2;
        while (s.size() < n) {
            int count = s[i];
            for (int j = 0; j < count; ++j) {
                s.push_back(current);
            }
            current = 3 - current;
            ++i;
        }
        int ones = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (s[i] == 1) ++ones;
        }
        return ones;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The algorithm generates the magical string iteratively by using the existing elements to determine how many of the next characters (alternating between 1 and 2) to append. This approach ensures linear time and space complexity, making it efficient for large n. The key strength is its direct simulation of the problem's rules, while the weakness is the O(n) space requirement.
